experiment: b=5 deep-region defect ceiling, tetraquadric 3+1 family
method: exact rank over F_p via flint.nmod_mat; vectorized sparse construction. The reported b=5 ceiling is stable across the listed primes for the recorded seed.
provenance: Defects are the established results of prior exact-F_p decisive runs (also recorded in results/saturation_onset.json). Matrix dimensions and ranks are derived from the build_sparse dimensions and the identity rank = min(s,t) - defect. The commands listed below in the reproduce field recompute these values when python-flint is installed.
matrix_convention: rows x cols = nt x len(S) from build_sparse((-d,-d,-d,5), p, seed); defect = min(len(S), nt) - rank

(14,14,14).note: last sub-ceiling depth below tau(5)=15; two primes; raw logs logs/b5_d14_p*.txt

(16,16,16).provenance: stored prior plateau check (two primes); NOT part of the raw-log archival set; recompute with scripts/make_decisive_logs.py --only 5 16 PRIME on a >4 GB host

verdict: Finite-field ranks at b=5 match the Thom-Porteous value 960 = 48*C(6,3) at the logged depth-15 two-prime computation; the stored depth-16 prior plateau check returns the same value (not part of the raw-log archive). The finite-field computation disagrees with the factorial value 6!*2^3 = 5760 by a factor of six. The general-b identification is proved in Theorem 5.1 for the acyclic Toeplitz range.
status: computed finite-field distinction between candidate ceilings; general-b identification is proved in Theorem 5.1 for the acyclic Toeplitz range
note: The (15) and (16) matrices are about 16k x 16k and 20k x 20k dense over F_p; the rank step needs roughly 2-3 GB and 15-30 minutes per prime on one core.
